Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
As to our translations of the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, yours was doing harm, and mine was necessary in self-defence. You should be glad that heresy is exposed.

title: As to our translations of the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, yours was doing harm, and mine was necessary in self-defence. You should be glad that heresy is exposed.

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “But why,” you ask, “did you accept my manuscripts which had been falsified? and why, when I had translated the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν did you dare to put your pen to the same work?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If I had erred, as any man may, ought you not to summon me to reply by a private letter, and to speak smoothly to me, as I am speaking smoothly in my present letter?”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: My whole fault is this that, when accusations were brought against me in the guise of disingenuous praise, I tried to purge myself from them, and this without invidiously introducing your name.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I wished to refer to many persons a charge which you alone had brought, not so as to retort the charge of heresy upon you, but to repel it from myself.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Could I know that you would be angry if I wrote against the heretics?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You had said that you had taken away the heretical passages from the works of Origen.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I therefore turned my attacks not upon you but upon the heretics, for I did not believe that you were a favourer of heresy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Pardon me, if I did this with too great vehemence.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I thought that I should give you pleasure.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say that it was by the dishonest tricks of those who acted for me that your manuscripts were brought out before the public, when they were kept secretly in your chamber, or were in possession only of the man who had desired to have the translation made for him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But how is this reconcilable with your former statement that either no one or very few had them?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If they were kept secret in your chamber, how could they be in the possession of the man who had desired to have the translation made for him?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If the one man for whom the manuscripts had been written had obtained them in order to conceal them, then they were not kept secret in your chamber, and they were not in the hands of those few who, as you now declare, possessed them.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You accuse us of having stolen them away; and then again you reproach us with having bought them for a great sum of money and an immense bribe.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In a single matter, and in one little letter, what a tissue of various and discordant falsehoods!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You have full liberty for accusation, but I have none for defence.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: When you bring a charge, you think nothing about friendship.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: When I begin to reply, then your mind is full of the rights of friendship.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Let me ask you: Did you write these manuscripts for concealment or for publication?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If for concealment, why were they written?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If for publication, why did you conceal them?
